_meta.authoringNotes: Epidemiology from Yao et al. J Clin Oncol 2008 (PMID 18165649). Somatostatin analogue efficacy from PROMID (Rinke et al. 2009 PMID 19546158) and CLARINET (Caplin et al. 2014 PMID 25213101) trials. Carcinoid heart disease from Moller et al. J Am Coll Cardiol 2020. ENETS 2016 consensus guidelines for management.

h1Title: Carcinoid Syndrome: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Carcinoid syndrome is a clinical syndrome caused by hormones — principally serotonin, histamine, tachykinins, and prostaglandins — secreted by neuroendocrine tumors (NETs) directly into the systemic circulation. It arises in roughly 10% of patients with neuroendocrine tumors, almost always once hepatic metastases allow secreted products to bypass first-pass liver metabolism. The cardinal features are episodic facial flushing (in 90% of cases), secretory diarrhea, and bronchospasm. Carcinoid heart disease — fibrotic plaques on the right-sided heart valves — develops in up to 20-50% of patients with untreated syndrome and is a leading cause of death. Somatostatin analogues (octreotide, lanreotide) control symptoms in the majority of patients and have demonstrated antiproliferative activity in well-differentiated NETs.

keyStats.prevalence: Neuroendocrine tumors affect approximately 6.98 per 100,000 persons annually (SEER database, Yao et al. 2008); carcinoid syndrome develops in roughly 10% of all NET patients
keyStats.demographics: Slight female predominance in small-bowel NETs; peak incidence age 50-70; no strong ethnic predisposition, though appendiceal NETs are more common in younger adults
keyStats.avgAge: Median age at diagnosis of small-bowel NET (the most common site causing syndrome) is approximately 62 years
keyStats.globalCases: NET incidence has risen 5-fold over the past four decades, likely due to improved detection; estimated 170,000 Americans living with NETs in 2017 (Dasari et al.)

definition: Carcinoid syndrome (ICD-10: E34.0) is the systemic syndrome produced by excess secretion of bioactive amines and peptides — primarily serotonin (5-hydroxytryptamine), but also histamine, kallikrein, tachykinins, and prostaglandins — from neuroendocrine tumors. It is distinct from the tumor itself; a patient can have a carcinoid tumor without carcinoid syndrome. The syndrome occurs almost exclusively when NETs have metastasized to the liver, because hepatic metastases release their products directly into hepatic veins, bypassing first-pass inactivation that normally occurs when mediators drain via the portal circulation through the liver. Rare exceptions include extra-abdominal primary sites (lung, ovary) that drain directly into the systemic circulation. The most common primary tumor sites are the ileum and proximal jejunum (45% of functional NETs), followed by the appendix, rectum, and pancreas. NETs are classified by grade (G1, G2, G3) based on Ki-67 proliferative index and mitotic rate; carcinoid syndrome most often accompanies well-differentiated G1-G2 tumors. Endocrinology, gastroenterology, and oncology collaborate in management; carcinoid heart disease involves cardiology.

typesClassification.description: Small-bowel and proximal colonic NETs secreting predominantly serotonin. Produces the full syndrome: episodic flushing, secretory diarrhea, bronchospasm, and carcinoid heart disease. Urine 5-HIAA is markedly elevated. Most common in ileal NETs with liver metastases.

Bronchial and gastric NETs secrete histamine rather than serotonin as the principal mediator. Flushing is prolonged (hours to days), often more intensely red, and associated with lacrimation, hypotension, and periorbital edema. 5-HIAA may be only mildly elevated; histamine metabolites in urine are useful markers.

A life-threatening exacerbation during anesthesia, procedures, or stress, with profound flushing, bronchospasm, hypotension, and tachycardia from sudden massive hormone release. Prevented and treated with IV octreotide bolus before any procedure.

Fibrotic plaques on the endocardium of the right-sided heart valves (tricuspid regurgitation, pulmonary stenosis) caused by circulating serotonin that bypasses pulmonary inactivation. Develops in 20-50% of untreated patients; a leading cause of NET-related mortality.

primarySymptoms: Episodic facial and neck flushing (in 90% of patients) — typically brief (2-5 minutes), dry and warm, triggered by food, alcohol, emotional stress, or physical exertion, and followed by normal skin color.
Secretory diarrhea with 5-10 or more watery stools per day, often accompanied by urgency and abdominal cramping, caused by serotonin-driven intestinal hypermotility and fluid secretion.
Intermittent bronchospasm producing wheeze and dyspnea, particularly during flushing episodes, from serotonin and bradykinin-mediated bronchoconstriction.
Abdominal pain and cramping from bowel hypermotility, small-bowel mesenteric fibrosis (a tumor-related complication), or intermittent bowel obstruction.
Peripheral edema and ascites from right-sided heart failure caused by carcinoid heart disease with tricuspid regurgitation.
Weight loss and malnutrition from chronic diarrhea, reduced appetite, and serotonin's diversion of tryptophan away from protein synthesis toward serotonin production.
Niacin (B3) deficiency causing pellagra-like dermatitis — a late complication when excess tryptophan is used for serotonin synthesis, leaving insufficient substrate for niacin production.
Telangiectasia (dilated surface vessels) on the face after repeated flushing episodes, resembling rosacea but more diffuse.
Hypotension and tachycardia during carcinoid crises — sudden life-threatening cardiovascular instability triggered by procedures, stress, or anesthesia.
Fatigue, exertional dyspnea, and new murmur from right-sided valvular disease in established carcinoid heart disease — a late but serious finding.
earlyWarningSigns: Episodic facial flushing lasting 2-5 minutes without clear dermatological cause, particularly if triggered by food, alcohol, or stress.
Chronic, watery, non-bloody diarrhea with negative stool cultures and colonoscopy that fails to reveal an inflammatory cause.
Unexplained urinary 5-HIAA elevation above 50 µmol/24 h on a routine or incidental urine metabolite screen.
Incidental finding of a liver or mesenteric mass on imaging performed for an unrelated indication.
New tricuspid regurgitation murmur in a patient with known NET — a signal that carcinoid heart disease may be developing.
emergencySigns: Carcinoid crisis: sudden profound hypotension, bronchospasm, flushing, and confusion during anesthesia, endoscopy, biopsy, or other procedure — treat with IV octreotide 500 µg bolus immediately and avoid catecholamines.
Bowel obstruction from mesenteric fibrosis or the primary ileal tumor, presenting with severe colicky pain, vomiting, and radiographic small-bowel obstruction requiring urgent surgical evaluation.
Acute right-sided heart failure with cardiogenic shock from advanced tricuspid regurgitation and pulmonary stenosis in carcinoid heart disease.
Pellagra with severe dermatitis, diarrhea, and dementia in a patient with advanced metastatic NET — indicates critical niacin depletion requiring immediate supplementation.

causes.cause: Serotonin excess from hepatic metastases of ileal neuroendocrine tumors
causes.description: Ileal NETs are the most common cause of carcinoid syndrome. When liver metastases develop, serotonin produced by tumor cells bypasses portal circulation and enters the systemic bloodstream. Serotonin stimulates intestinal motility (diarrhea) and binds receptors in the heart and lung. The severity of syndrome correlates with tumor burden and liver metastasis volume.

causes.cause: Histamine excess from gastric and bronchial NETs
causes.description: Foregut NETs (stomach, bronchus, thymus) preferentially produce histamine rather than serotonin. Histamine causes prolonged, bright-red flushing that is more intense than typical serotonin-mediated flushing. Urinary histamine and its metabolite methylimidazole acetic acid are elevated, while 5-HIAA may be only mildly raised.

Substance P, neuropeptide K, and kallikrein (which generates bradykinin) are co-secreted by many NETs and mediate bronchoconstriction, vasodilation, and potentiation of diarrhea. Bradykinin is a major trigger of severe flushing and hypotension during carcinoid crisis.

Some NETs secrete prostaglandins that cause vasodilation and diarrhea. Prostaglandins contribute especially to flushing in gastric NETs and may explain why some patients respond to aspirin or indomethacin (which inhibit prostaglandin synthesis) in addition to somatostatin analogues.

causes.cause: Extra-abdominal primaries draining directly to systemic circulation
causes.description: Bronchial carcinoids (lung NETs), ovarian NETs, and extremely large primary abdominal NETs can cause carcinoid syndrome without liver metastases because their venous drainage bypasses portal first-pass inactivation. Bronchial carcinoids account for about 10% of carcinoid syndrome cases.

riskFactors.factor: Hepatic metastases from a neuroendocrine tumor
riskFactors.description: The most important risk factor for developing carcinoid syndrome; syndrome almost always requires liver metastases from intestinal NETs. At diagnosis, approximately 40-50% of small-bowel NET patients already have liver metastases.

MEN1 syndrome (mutation in the menin gene) predisposes to pancreatic NETs, including serotonin-secreting tumors, as well as pituitary adenomas and hyperparathyroidism. MEN1-associated NETs tend to be multifocal.
Ileal NETs are the most likely to be functional and produce carcinoid syndrome because they have high serotonin-secreting capacity and frequent early lymph node and liver metastasis.

riskFactors.factor: High tumor burden and elevated chromogranin A
riskFactors.description: Larger tumor volume, multiple liver metastases, and a markedly elevated plasma chromogranin A (above 300 µg/L) correlate with higher circulating serotonin and more severe carcinoid syndrome.

Any procedure that manipulates or reduces tumor blood flow — including biopsy, embolization, ablation, or general anesthesia — can precipitate carcinoid crisis if IV octreotide is not given prophylactically. Risk is highest with hepatic arterial embolization.

Foods rich in serotonin or tyramine — nuts, chocolate, aged cheeses, alcohol, avocado — and medications that release serotonin (epinephrine, norepinephrine) can trigger flushing and diarrhea by directly stimulating hormone release from tumor.

affectedDemographics: Small-bowel NETs have a slight female predominance and occur most often between the ages of 50-70 years. Appendiceal NETs are more common under 40 years.
No strong racial or geographic predisposition for carcinoid syndrome; NET incidence appears similar across European, North American, and Asian populations in population-based registries.
MEN1 mutation carriers (1 in 30,000) have a markedly elevated lifetime risk of pancreatic NETs that can cause carcinoid syndrome.
Bronchial carcinoids causing atypical carcinoid syndrome have a stronger female predominance and an earlier age of onset (mean 45 years) compared with midgut NETs.
diagnosisOverview: Carcinoid syndrome is confirmed by demonstrating elevated levels of serotonin metabolites — primarily 24-hour urinary 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid (5-HIAA) — alongside tumor localization by imaging. The 24-hour urine 5-HIAA test is the gold standard with sensitivity of 70-75% and specificity above 90% for carcinoid syndrome; it requires a 3-day dietary restriction of serotonin-containing foods (walnuts, bananas, tomatoes) before collection. Plasma 5-HIAA assays are emerging as a more convenient alternative with comparable accuracy. Plasma chromogranin A (CgA) is the most widely used general NET marker — elevated above 300 µg/L in advanced disease — though it is non-specific (also elevated with proton pump inhibitor use, heart failure, and renal failure). Somatostatin receptor scintigraphy with 68Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT is the most sensitive imaging for NET localization and staging, detecting lesions as small as 3-5 mm and identifying both hepatic and extra-hepatic metastases. CT or MRI of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis defines anatomical disease burden. Echocardiography is performed in all patients with carcinoid syndrome to detect and grade carcinoid heart disease, and is repeated annually. Tissue biopsy confirms histology and provides Ki-67 grade essential for treatment planning. European Neuroendocrine Tumor Society (ENETS) 2016 consensus guidelines define the staging and management pathway.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard biochemical marker for carcinoid syndrome; measures the primary serotonin metabolite
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Collect all urine over 24 hours in a provided container. Avoid walnuts, bananas, kiwi, pineapple, and serotonergic medications for 3 days before collection. Normal range below 50 µmol/24 h; values above 100 µmol/24 h are strongly suggestive of carcinoid syndrome. Results in 1-3 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: General neuroendocrine tumor marker reflecting overall tumor burden; useful for monitoring response to therapy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw, fasting. Stop proton pump inhibitors 2 weeks before if possible, as PPIs cause false elevations. Results in 2-5 days. Normal below 94 µg/L in most assays.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Sensitive staging and localization of somatostatin receptor-positive NETs throughout the body
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A radiotracer injection followed by a 60-minute wait and then a 30-minute PET/CT scan. Outpatient procedure; mild radiation exposure. More sensitive than conventional octreotide scintigraphy; superior to CT alone for small mesenteric nodes and bone metastases.

diagnosticTests.test: CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast
diagnosticTests.purpose: Anatomical staging of primary tumor and metastases; guides surgery, ablation, and liver-directed therapy planning
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard CT with IV contrast; 15-20 minutes. Identifies primary ileal tumor as a soft-tissue mass with desmoplastic reaction, liver metastases, and mesenteric lymph nodes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects and grades carcinoid heart disease — tricuspid regurgitation, pulmonary stenosis, right heart dilatation
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A painless 30-minute ultrasound scan. Repeated annually in patients with active carcinoid syndrome; more frequently if valve disease is detected. Establishes whether valve surgery is needed.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Tissue diagnosis confirming neuroendocrine histology, grade (Ki-67, mitotic index), and somatostatin receptor expression
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Colonoscopy for rectal or colonic NET; enteroscopy or capsule endoscopy for small-bowel lesions; CT-guided biopsy for liver metastases. Local anesthesia and sedation. Pathology results in 5-10 days; Ki-67 index determines G1 (below 2%), G2 (2-20%), or G3 (above 20%) classification.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of carcinoid syndrome targets both symptom control and tumor growth control, as the two are intertwined — larger tumor burden produces more hormone and worsens symptoms. Somatostatin analogues (SSAs) are the cornerstone: octreotide LAR (20-30 mg IM monthly) and lanreotide autogel (120 mg SC every 4 weeks) both control symptoms in 60-70% of patients and have demonstrated statistically significant progression-free survival benefit in PROMID and CLARINET trials, respectively. For breakthrough symptoms on long-acting SSA, short-acting subcutaneous octreotide (100-500 µg as needed) is self-administered. Telotristat ethyl — an oral tryptophan hydroxylase inhibitor — reduces serotonin synthesis in the gut and, at 250 mg three times daily, reduces the frequency of daily bowel movements by more than 30% in patients whose diarrhea is not controlled by SSA alone (TELESTAR trial, 2017). For antiproliferative therapy beyond SSAs: everolimus (mTOR inhibitor, 10 mg/day) and sunitinib (37.5 mg/day) have phase III trial data for pancreatic NETs; everolimus has data for non-pancreatic NETs (RADIANT-4). Peptide receptor radionuclide therapy (PRRT) with 177Lu-DOTATATE (Lutathera) achieves a 65% progression-free survival rate at 20 months versus 11% for high-dose octreotide in the NETTER-1 trial and is the preferred treatment for progressive, somatostatin-receptor-positive NETs. Hepatic-directed therapies — surgical resection of liver metastases, liver transplantation in select patients, hepatic artery embolization, and radiofrequency ablation — can dramatically reduce tumor burden and hormone secretion. Carcinoid heart disease requiring valve surgery is managed by valve repair or bioprosthetic valve replacement, performed when right ventricular function is still preserved. Carcinoid crisis is prevented by giving IV octreotide 500 µg bolus before all procedures in patients with known carcinoid syndrome.

medicalTreatments.name: Octreotide LAR (20-30 mg IM every 4 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Long-acting somatostatin analogue that suppresses serotonin secretion and inhibits tumor growth by binding somatostatin receptors 2 and 5. First-line for symptom control and antiproliferative therapy in functional NETs.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom control (diarrhea and flushing) in 60-70% of patients; time to tumor progression 14.3 months vs 6 months with placebo in PROMID trial (Rinke et al. 2009)

medicalTreatments.name: Lanreotide autogel (120 mg SC every 4 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative long-acting SSA with similar efficacy to octreotide LAR; self-administered by patient or nurse. CLARINET trial demonstrated significantly improved progression-free survival in G1/G2 non-pancreatic NETs.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Stable disease or tumor response in 65.1% vs 33.0% with placebo at 24 months; progression-free survival not reached vs 18 months with placebo (Caplin et al. 2014)

medicalTreatments.name: Telotristat ethyl 250 mg three times daily (oral)
medicalTreatments.description: Peripheral tryptophan hydroxylase-1 inhibitor that reduces gut serotonin synthesis without CNS effects. Added to SSA in patients with inadequately controlled diarrhea. Not for flushing, which is mainly serotonin-independent.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduced daily bowel movements by 1.7 per day vs 0.9 with placebo in TELESTAR trial; 44% of patients achieved at least 30% reduction sustained over 12 weeks

medicalTreatments.name: 177Lu-DOTATATE (Lutetium-177 PRRT, Lutathera)
medicalTreatments.description: Peptide receptor radionuclide therapy that delivers beta radiation to somatostatin-receptor-expressing tumor cells. Given as four infusions at 8-week intervals in specialized nuclear medicine centers.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Progression-free survival 65.2% at 20 months vs 10.8% with high-dose octreotide in NETTER-1 trial (Strosberg et al. 2017); approved by FDA and EMA for somatostatin-receptor-positive gastroenteropancreatic NETs

medicalTreatments.name: Everolimus (10 mg oral daily)
medicalTreatments.description: mTOR inhibitor with antiproliferative activity in NETs. RADIANT-4 trial demonstrated benefit in non-pancreatic, non-functional NETs; RADIANT-3 in pancreatic NETs.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Median progression-free survival 11.0 months vs 3.9 months with placebo in RADIANT-4; 11.0 vs 4.6 months in RADIANT-3

medicalTreatments.name: Short-acting octreotide 100-500 µg SC (breakthrough dosing)
medicalTreatments.description: Self-injected for rescue of breakthrough flushing or diarrhea episodes between long-acting SSA doses and for prevention of carcinoid crisis before procedures.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Acute symptom relief within 15-30 minutes in the majority of patients; IV 500 µg bolus is the treatment of choice during carcinoid crisis

surgicalOptions.name: Resection of primary small-bowel NET and regional lymph nodes
surgicalOptions.description: Surgery is curative for localized disease and prevents mesenteric fibrosis, bowel obstruction, and ischemia even in metastatic disease. Most patients with ileal NETs have mesenteric lymph node involvement that should be resected en bloc.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 10-year survival above 75% for locoregional disease; resection in metastatic disease is cytoreductive, reducing hormone burden and improving symptom control

surgicalOptions.name: Hepatic metastasis resection or ablation
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical resection of liver metastases is recommended when more than 70-90% debulking is achievable. Radiofrequency ablation, microwave ablation, and hepatic arterial embolization are used for unresectable hepatic disease.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Five-year survival 60-80% after R0 liver resection in selected series; symptom control achieved in 90-95% when debulking exceeds 90%

surgicalOptions.name: Bioprosthetic valve replacement (carcinoid heart disease)
surgicalOptions.description: Tricuspid and pulmonary valve replacement with tissue (not mechanical) prostheses when symptomatic severe right-sided valve disease is present and right ventricular function is preserved. Performed after optimizing carcinoid syndrome with SSA.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Operative mortality approximately 10-15% in published series; 5-year survival above 60% in carefully selected patients; quality of life improves significantly post-operatively

alternativeTreatments.name: Interferon-alpha
alternativeTreatments.description: Second-line antiproliferative therapy for carcinoid syndrome unresponsive to SSA. Reduces flushing and diarrhea in 40-50% of patients. Side-effect profile (fatigue, flu-like symptoms, depression) limits long-term use.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary modification and trigger avoidance
alternativeTreatments.description: Avoiding serotonin-rich foods (nuts, avocado, pineapple, banana), alcohol, aged cheese, and medications that stimulate serotonin release reduces flush and diarrhea frequency. An evidence-based adjunct to pharmacological management.

whySeeSpecialist: Carcinoid syndrome requires multidisciplinary specialist management. An endocrinologist or NET specialist establishes the diagnosis, coordinates tumor staging, and initiates somatostatin analogue therapy. A hepatobiliary surgeon evaluates liver-directed therapy candidacy. A cardiologist performs and monitors echocardiography for carcinoid heart disease. Nuclear medicine provides PRRT.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a center with a dedicated NET multidisciplinary team (MDT) — ENETS-certified centers of excellence in Europe, or NANETS-affiliated centers in North America. Volume of NET cases managed per year predicts better surgical, medical, and PRRT outcomes. Avoid centers that do not perform routine echocardiographic surveillance.
hospitalCriteria: 68Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT and somatostatin receptor imaging capability
Hepatobiliary surgery service experienced in NET debulking and liver resection
Nuclear medicine PRRT program with 177Lu-DOTATATE availability
Echocardiography service experienced in carcinoid heart disease grading
Access to telotristat ethyl and IV octreotide for crisis prevention
keyFacilities: ENETS-certified or NANETS-affiliated NET centers of excellence
Hepatobiliary surgical units with NET experience
Cardiologic services for carcinoid heart disease surveillance and surgery
preventionStrategies: Pre-medicate with IV octreotide 500 µg bolus at least 30 minutes before any procedure in a patient with known carcinoid syndrome to prevent carcinoid crisis.

Avoid dietary triggers (nuts, avocado, pineapple, banana, alcohol, aged cheeses) and drugs that stimulate serotonin release (catecholamines, epinephrine) to reduce frequency and severity of flushing and diarrhea episodes.
Maintain somatostatin analogue therapy without gap — lapses in LAR injection schedule allow serotonin rebound and symptom flares.
Perform annual echocardiography in all patients with active carcinoid syndrome to detect carcinoid heart disease at an operable stage.
Screen first-degree relatives of MEN1 mutation carriers for neuroendocrine tumors to detect disease before carcinoid syndrome develops.
lifestyleModifications: Take long-acting SSA injections on schedule; gaps of more than 5-6 weeks allow serotonin levels to rise and symptoms to recur.
Carry short-acting octreotide (self-injection kit) for breakthrough symptoms and before any medical or dental procedure.
Wear a medical alert bracelet identifying carcinoid syndrome and contraindication to catecholamines (epinephrine, norepinephrine) as vasopressors.
Eat small, frequent meals and avoid large portions of serotonin-rich foods; keep a food and symptom diary to identify personal triggers.
Supplement niacin (vitamin B3) if pellagra symptoms develop — consult the care team for appropriate dose.
Attend all scheduled NET surveillance imaging and tumor marker blood tests, typically every 3-6 months in active disease.

recommended: Small, frequent low-fat meals to minimize gut hypermotility and postprandial flushing
Adequate protein from low-serotonin sources (chicken, fish without high tryptophan content) to counter serotonin-related protein depletion
Niacin-containing foods (lean meat, poultry, peanuts) to prevent pellagra from tryptophan diversion
Adequate hydration to replace fluid lost through diarrhea
avoid: Walnuts, pecans, cashews, and most tree nuts — high-serotonin foods that worsen flushing and diarrhea
Alcohol, particularly red wine and beer, which trigger serotonin release from tumors
Avocado, pineapple, banana, kiwi, and tomatoes — high-serotonin content foods to restrict before 5-HIAA testing and when symptomatic
Aged cheeses and fermented foods containing tyramine, which stimulates catecholamine release and can precipitate flushing

exerciseGuidelines: Light to moderate aerobic exercise is safe and recommended for most patients with well-controlled carcinoid syndrome. Vigorous exertion can trigger flushing and should be approached cautiously, particularly in patients with carcinoid heart disease or poorly controlled symptoms. Patients should take short-acting octreotide before anticipated vigorous physical activity if flushing is a consistent trigger.
dailyManagement: Self-monitor daily bowel movement frequency and character — worsening diarrhea above 4-5 stools/day may indicate tumor progression or inadequate SSA dosing.
Record all flushing episodes: frequency, duration, triggers, and associated symptoms to inform the clinical team at review.
Keep a short-acting octreotide injection kit available at all times for breakthrough symptoms or before planned procedures.
Take prescribed telotristat ethyl tablets three times daily with food if added to SSA for diarrhea control.
Monitor weight weekly — weight loss signals worsening disease activity or nutritional compromise.
Attend 3-6 monthly tumor marker (CgA, 5-HIAA) and imaging surveillance appointments.
prognosis: Prognosis in carcinoid syndrome is primarily determined by NET grade and tumor burden. Well-differentiated G1 NETs with carcinoid syndrome have a median 5-year survival of 56-77% in population-based registries (Yao et al. 2008, SEER data). Patients with locoregional disease only have 5-year survival exceeding 90%; those with distant metastases have 5-year survival of approximately 39-67% depending on primary site and treatment era. The NETTER-1 trial demonstrated that PRRT extends progression-free survival by approximately 20 months in somatostatin-receptor-positive disease. Carcinoid heart disease significantly reduces survival if untreated — right heart failure accounts for 25-50% of NET-related deaths in some series; timely valve surgery restores quality of life and extends survival. Telotristat ethyl and modern combination strategies have improved diarrhea control and nutritional status, reducing complication deaths from electrolyte depletion and malnutrition.
recoveryTimeline: Symptom response to somatostatin analogues begins within 24-72 hours of first injection; full symptomatic benefit is assessed at 3 months. PRRT requires 4 cycles over 32 weeks, with tumor response assessed at 3 months post-treatment. Valve surgery recovery is typically 6-12 weeks. Liver-directed therapy (resection or ablation) produces biochemical and symptomatic responses within 4-8 weeks.
complications: Carcinoid heart disease with tricuspid regurgitation and pulmonary stenosis, developing in 20-50% of patients with untreated syndrome — screen with annual echocardiography.
Pellagra (niacin deficiency) from chronic tryptophan diversion to serotonin synthesis, causing dermatitis, diarrhea, and dementia — supplement niacin if suspected.
Mesenteric fibrosis from desmoplastic tumor reaction around the primary small-bowel tumor, causing bowel obstruction, mesenteric ischemia, and superior mesenteric vessel encasement.
Malnutrition and electrolyte depletion from chronic secretory diarrhea — hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, and protein-calorie malnutrition without nutritional support.
Carcinoid crisis — anesthesia or procedural-related life-threatening hemodynamic collapse if octreotide prophylaxis is omitted.
Second primary tumors: patients with ileal NETs have a 15-25% risk of a synchronous second gastrointestinal or other primary cancer.

supportResources.name: Carcinoid Cancer Foundation
supportResources.description: US patient and research organization providing educational resources, clinical trial information, and patient support for carcinoid and neuroendocrine tumors.

supportResources.name: European Neuroendocrine Tumor Society (ENETS) — Patient Resources
supportResources.description: Access to ENETS-certified NET centers of excellence and patient guides on neuroendocrine tumor management.

supportResources.name: NET Patient Foundation (UK)
supportResources.description: UK charity supporting people with neuroendocrine tumors, offering information on carcinoid syndrome, octreotide, and specialist NET centers.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hypothyroidism
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hypothyroidism causes chronic fatigue, weight gain, and bradycardia — the opposite metabolic state to the flushing and diarrhea of carcinoid syndrome. TSH is elevated in hypothyroidism; 5-HIAA is normal. Distinguishing the two requires thyroid function tests and urine metabolites.

confusedWithConditions.name: Heart failure
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Right-sided heart failure from carcinoid heart disease can be confused with other causes of heart failure. Carcinoid heart disease specifically affects the right-sided valves (tricuspid regurgitation, pulmonary stenosis) and occurs in the setting of elevated 5-HIAA. Standard heart failure affects left-sided function first and lacks the neuroendocrine marker profile.

confusedWithConditions.name: Goiter
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Thyroid goiter and medullary thyroid carcinoma (which is a NET secreting calcitonin) can both present with neck mass and neuroendocrine markers. Medullary thyroid carcinoma is distinguished by elevated calcitonin and CEA rather than 5-HIAA, and does not classically cause flushing from serotonin.

relatedConditions.name: Pituitary adenoma
relatedConditions.relevance: MEN1 syndrome causes both pancreatic NETs (which can produce carcinoid syndrome) and pituitary adenomas, making them co-occurring in the same genetic syndrome.

relatedConditions.name: Acromegaly
relatedConditions.relevance: Both acromegaly and carcinoid syndrome involve excess hormone secretion by neuroendocrine tumors and are both managed with somatostatin analogues as first-line therapy.

relatedConditions.name: Lymphoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Carcinoid heart disease and the need for valve surgery occasionally coexist with lymphoma presentations in the differential for right-sided cardiac and mediastinal masses.

relatedConditions.name: Goiter
relatedConditions.relevance: Medullary thyroid carcinoma — a neuroendocrine tumor of thyroid C cells — is in the differential for neuroendocrine syndromes with flushing, and both can coexist in MEN2 syndrome.

faqs.question: What is carcinoid syndrome?
faqs.answer: Carcinoid syndrome is a set of symptoms — flushing, diarrhea, and wheeze — caused by hormones secreted by a neuroendocrine tumor (NET) directly into the bloodstream. It occurs almost always when a NET has spread to the liver, allowing serotonin and other mediators to bypass liver breakdown.

faqs.question: What causes flushing in carcinoid syndrome?
faqs.answer: Flushing is triggered by serotonin, histamine, and bradykinin released by the neuroendocrine tumor. Episodes last 2-5 minutes, typically affect the face and neck, and are provoked by food, alcohol, emotional stress, or physical exertion. Foregut NETs cause longer, more intense flushes mediated by histamine.

faqs.question: How is carcinoid syndrome diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis relies on 24-hour urine 5-HIAA (gold-standard biochemical marker), plasma chromogranin A, and 68Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT for tumor localization. Biopsy confirms NET histology and Ki-67 grade. Echocardiography screens for carcinoid heart disease at diagnosis and annually thereafter.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for carcinoid syndrome?
faqs.answer: Somatostatin analogues — octreotide LAR or lanreotide — control symptoms in 60-70% of patients and slow tumor growth. Telotristat ethyl is added for refractory diarrhea. Surgical debulking, PRRT with 177Lu-DOTATATE, and hepatic-directed therapy are used when tumors progress.

faqs.question: What is a carcinoid crisis?
faqs.answer: Carcinoid crisis is a life-threatening surge of hormones triggered during procedures, anesthesia, or stress, causing severe hypotension, bronchospasm, and flushing. It is treated with IV octreotide 500 µg immediately. All patients with carcinoid syndrome must receive IV octreotide prophylaxis before any procedure.

faqs.question: Can carcinoid syndrome affect the heart?
faqs.answer: Yes. Carcinoid heart disease develops in 20-50% of patients with untreated syndrome. Serotonin deposits fibrous plaques on the right-sided heart valves, causing tricuspid regurgitation and pulmonary stenosis. Annual echocardiography is mandatory. Severe valve disease requires surgical bioprosthetic replacement.

faqs.question: What foods should be avoided with carcinoid syndrome?
faqs.answer: Avoid serotonin-rich foods including walnuts, avocado, pineapple, banana, and kiwi; also alcohol, aged cheeses, and fermented foods. These trigger flushing and diarrhea by stimulating hormone release from the tumor. Also avoid epinephrine and norepinephrine as vasopressors, which can trigger carcinoid crisis.

faqs.question: What is 5-HIAA and why is it tested?
faqs.answer: 5-HIAA (5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid) is the main breakdown product of serotonin, excreted in urine. A 24-hour urine 5-HIAA above 100 µmol/day strongly suggests carcinoid syndrome. Patients must avoid serotonin-rich foods for 3 days before collection. It is also used to monitor treatment response.

faqs.question: How long can you live with carcinoid syndrome?
faqs.answer: Prognosis depends on grade and stage. Well-differentiated G1 NETs with liver metastases have a 5-year survival of approximately 56-77% in SEER registry data. Localized disease has 5-year survival exceeding 90%. Modern PRRT extends progression-free survival significantly in somatostatin-receptor-positive disease.

faqs.question: Is carcinoid syndrome the same as a carcinoid tumor?
faqs.answer: No. A carcinoid tumor (neuroendocrine tumor) is the underlying growth. Carcinoid syndrome refers specifically to the symptoms caused by hormones secreted by the tumor entering the systemic circulation. Only about 10% of patients with neuroendocrine tumors develop carcinoid syndrome, usually after liver metastasis.

faqs.question: Does octreotide cure carcinoid syndrome?
faqs.answer: Octreotide controls symptoms in 60-70% of patients but does not cure the underlying neuroendocrine tumor. Long-acting SSA also slows tumor growth. If tumors progress on SSA, PRRT or other systemic therapies are added. Surgical debulking can dramatically reduce hormone secretion when feasible.

faqs.question: Can carcinoid syndrome cause vitamin deficiency?
faqs.answer: Yes. Chronic serotonin overproduction diverts tryptophan away from niacin (vitamin B3) synthesis. Niacin deficiency causes pellagra — a triad of dermatitis, diarrhea, and dementia. Niacin supplementation is used once pellagra symptoms are detected, and telotristat reduces serotonin production, sparing tryptophan.

faqs.question: What is PRRT and is it used for carcinoid syndrome?
faqs.answer: Peptide receptor radionuclide therapy (PRRT) with 177Lu-DOTATATE (Lutathera) delivers targeted radiation to somatostatin-receptor-expressing NETs. NETTER-1 trial showed 65% progression-free survival at 20 months versus 11% with high-dose octreotide. It is FDA and EMA approved for gastroenteropancreatic NETs.

faqs.question: What triggers carcinoid flushing?
faqs.answer: Common triggers include alcohol, serotonin-rich foods (walnuts, avocado, banana), emotional stress, vigorous exercise, and certain drugs (catecholamines, monoamine oxidase inhibitors). Temperature changes and large meals can also provoke episodes. Identifying and avoiding personal triggers reduces flush frequency.

faqs.question: Is carcinoid syndrome hereditary?
faqs.answer: Most cases are sporadic. MEN1 syndrome (menin gene mutation) predisposes to pancreatic NETs, which can cause carcinoid syndrome, and is hereditary. Genetic testing for MEN1 is recommended when multiple endocrine tumors are present or when family history suggests MEN1.

metaDescription: Carcinoid syndrome: flushing, diarrhea and wheezing from hormones released by a neuroendocrine tumor. Symptoms, diagnosis and treatment reviewed.
